title: Roasted Aubergine with Buttermilk Sauce and Pomegranate
intro: Charred aubergine halves crowned with a cool, tangy buttermilk pour and jewels of pomegranate. It is the kind of showpiece starter that looks far more effort than it takes.
steps: Heat the oven to 200C and score the cut face of each aubergine half in a deep diamond pattern.
Brush generously with olive oil, scatter over thyme, salt and pepper, then roast cut-side up for 35 minutes until deeply golden and collapsing.
Whisk the buttermilk, yogurt, garlic and lemon juice together with a pinch of salt to make a loose, pourable sauce.
Let the roasted aubergines cool to just warm so the sauce will not slide straight off.
Spoon the buttermilk sauce generously over each half.
Shower with pomegranate seeds and a heavy dusting of za'atar, then finish with a thread of olive oil.
tip: Roast the aubergines until they look almost too dark; that near-burnt edge is where the smoky flavour lives.

title: Spiced Cauliflower and Chickpea Traybake
intro: One tin, big flavours, almost no washing up. Turmeric-stained cauliflower and crisping chickpeas come together into a weeknight dinner with real backbone.
ingredients: 1 lemon, half juiced and half in wedges
steps: Heat the oven to 220C and tip the cauliflower, chickpeas and onion into a large roasting tin.
Add the oil, turmeric, cumin, coriander, chilli flakes and a good pinch of salt, then toss until everything is evenly coated.
Spread out in a single layer so the vegetables roast rather than steam.
Roast for 35 to 40 minutes, turning once halfway, until the cauliflower is tender and browned at the edges.
Squeeze over the lemon juice while everything is still hot.
Drizzle with coconut yogurt, scatter with fresh coriander and serve with the lemon wedges.
tip: Pat the chickpeas thoroughly dry before roasting so they crisp up instead of going soft.

title: Saag Paneer
intro: Soft cubes of golden paneer folded through a silky, deeply spiced spinach sauce. This is comfort food that rewards a little patience at the stove.
steps: Wilt the spinach in a dry pan or with a splash of water, then blitz to a coarse puree and set aside.
Fry the paneer cubes in a tablespoon of ghee until golden on all sides, then lift out and reserve.
In the same pan soften the onion in the remaining ghee, then add the garlic, ginger and chilli and cook until fragrant.
Stir in the cumin, garam masala and turmeric and toast for a minute until the raw smell lifts.
Add the spinach puree with a splash of water and simmer gently for eight to ten minutes.
Return the paneer, stir through the cream, season with salt and warm through before serving.
tip: Soak the fried paneer in warm salted water for a few minutes to keep it pillowy soft in the sauce.

title: Roasted Beet and Farro Salad with Goat Cheese
intro: Earthy roasted beets, nutty farro and creamy goat cheese, dressed simply to let each ingredient speak. A celebration of the garden in a single bowl.
steps: Heat the oven to 200C, wrap the beets in foil and roast for about 45 minutes until a knife slides in easily.
Meanwhile simmer the farro in salted water for 25 to 30 minutes until tender but chewy, then drain.
Whisk the olive oil, vinegar, mustard and shallot into a bright dressing and season to taste.
Peel the cooled beets under cool water, then cut into bite-sized wedges.
Toss the warm farro with most of the dressing so it drinks up the flavour.
Fold in the beets, scatter over goat cheese, walnuts and parsley, then finish with the last of the dressing.
tip: Dress the farro while it is still warm; the grains absorb the vinaigrette far better than when cold.

title: Butternut Squash and Sage Risotto
intro: A gently sweet, autumnal risotto flecked with crisp sage and finished with a generous handful of Parmesan. Stir, sip your wine, and let it come together slowly.
ingredients: 500 g butternut squash, peeled and diced
steps: Roast the diced squash with a tablespoon of oil at 200C for 25 minutes, then mash half and keep half in cubes.
Fry the sage leaves in a little oil until crisp, then drain on paper and reserve for garnish.
Soften the onion and garlic in the remaining oil, then stir in the rice and toast for two minutes.
Pour in the wine and let it bubble away, then add hot stock a ladle at a time, stirring until each addition is absorbed.
After about 18 minutes the rice should be creamy and just tender; stir through the mashed and cubed squash.
Beat in the butter and Parmesan off the heat, season well and top each bowl with crisp sage.
tip: Keep your stock at a gentle simmer in a separate pan so it never shocks the rice with cold liquid.

title: Patatas Bravas with Smoky Tomato Sauce
intro: Crisp golden potato cubes under a smoky, garlicky tomato sauce and a cool garlic aioli. The tapas bar classic, made properly at home.
ingredients: 800 g potatoes, cut into 3 cm cubes
1 small garlic clove, crushed, for aioli
steps: Parboil the potato cubes in salted water for six minutes, then drain and steam-dry in the colander.
Toss with olive oil and salt, spread on a tray and roast at 220C for 30 minutes until crisp and golden.
Meanwhile soften the minced garlic in a little oil, add the smoked paprika and cayenne and stir for thirty seconds.
Pour in the tomatoes and vinegar, then simmer for fifteen minutes and blend smooth, seasoning to taste.
Stir the crushed garlic into the mayonnaise to make a quick aioli.
Pile the hot potatoes onto a plate, spoon over the bravas sauce and finish with drizzles of aioli.
tip: Do not crowd the roasting tray; give the potatoes room so every side turns crisp rather than steamy.

title: Tomato and Basil Tart
intro: Buttery puff pastry, a whisper of mustard, and sun-ripe tomatoes softening in the oven. Simple, seasonal and quietly perfect with a glass of something cold.
steps: Heat the oven to 200C and lay the pastry on a lined baking tray, scoring a two centimetre border around the edge.
Brush the border with beaten egg, then spread the mustard evenly inside the scored frame.
Scatter the cheese over the mustard and arrange the tomato slices on top in overlapping rows.
Drizzle with olive oil, scatter over thyme and season generously with salt and pepper.
Bake for 28 to 30 minutes until the pastry is puffed and deep golden and the tomatoes have softened.
Tear over fresh basil just before serving so it stays fragrant.
tip: Salt the tomato slices and let them drain on kitchen paper for ten minutes so the tart base stays crisp.

title: Wild Mushroom and Leek Galette
intro: A rustic free-form pastry folded around earthy mushrooms and sweet, slow-cooked leeks. Imperfect edges are part of its homemade charm.
steps: Rub the butter into the flour with a pinch of salt until crumbly, add ice water and bring together into a disc, then chill for thirty minutes.
Soften the leeks in olive oil over low heat for ten minutes, then add the mushrooms, garlic and thyme and cook until any liquid evaporates.
Season the filling well and let it cool, then stir the ricotta and Parmesan through it.
Roll the pastry into a rough thirty centimetre circle on a lined tray.
Pile the filling into the centre, leaving a wide border, then fold the edges up and over in loose pleats.
Brush the pastry with beaten egg and bake at 200C for 35 to 40 minutes until golden and crisp.
tip: Cook the mushrooms until they squeak dry in the pan; excess moisture is the enemy of a crisp galette base.

title: Shakshuka with Feta
intro: Eggs poached in a spiced, simmering tomato and pepper sauce, crowned with salty feta. Bring the pan straight to the table with plenty of bread for dipping.
steps: Soften the onion and peppers in olive oil over medium heat for about ten minutes until slumping and sweet.
Add the garlic, cumin, paprika and caraway and cook for another minute until fragrant.
Pour in the tomatoes, season, and simmer for ten minutes until thick and jammy.
Make six wells in the sauce and crack an egg into each one.
Cover and cook gently for six to eight minutes until the whites are set but the yolks still run.
Scatter over the feta and fresh coriander and serve straight from the pan.
tip: Keep the heat low once the eggs go in; a fierce simmer overcooks the yolks before the whites are ready.

title: Chana Masala
intro: A robust, tangy chickpea curry built on toasted whole spices and finished with a squeeze of lemon. Hearty enough to stand as a meal in its own right.
steps: Warm the oil and sizzle the cumin seeds until they crackle and smell toasty.
Add the onion and cook slowly until deep golden, then stir in the garlic and ginger for a minute.
Add the tomatoes with the coriander, turmeric and chilli powder and cook down to a thick paste.
Tip in the chickpeas with a splash of water and simmer for fifteen minutes so they soak up the flavour.
Lightly crush some of the chickpeas against the pan to thicken the sauce.
Stir through the garam masala and lemon juice, season with salt and serve hot.
tip: A pinch of dried mango powder or extra lemon at the end gives that signature bright, tangy finish.

title: Charred Broccoli with Chilli and Garlic
intro: Blistered tender-stem broccoli tossed in a punchy chilli, garlic and soy dressing. A fast, fiery side that easily steals the show from the main.
steps: Blanch the broccoli in boiling salted water for two minutes, then drain well and pat dry.
Heat the neutral oil in a wide pan until shimmering and lay the broccoli in a single layer.
Let it char undisturbed for a few minutes until blackened in spots, then turn and char the other side.
Push the broccoli aside, add the garlic and chillies and fry until fragrant but not burnt.
Whisk the soy, vinegar, maple and sesame oil, then pour it in and toss to coat everything.
Finish with sesame seeds and serve at once while glossy and hot.
tip: Make sure the broccoli is bone dry before it hits the oil, or it will steam instead of char.

title: Eggplant Parmesan
intro: Layers of tender aubergine, rich tomato sauce and molten mozzarella baked into a bubbling, golden bake. Rich, generous and worth every minute.
steps: Salt the aubergine slices and leave for twenty minutes, then pat dry and brush with oil.
Roast the slices at 220C for twenty minutes, turning once, until soft and golden.
Meanwhile soften the onion and garlic in oil, add the tomatoes and oregano and simmer for fifteen minutes into a thick sauce.
Season the sauce and stir in a handful of torn basil.
Layer aubergine, tomato sauce and mozzarella in a baking dish, repeating until everything is used.
Top with Parmesan and breadcrumbs, then bake at 200C for twenty-five minutes until bubbling and golden.
tip: Let the finished bake rest for ten minutes before cutting so the layers hold their shape on the plate.

title: Halloumi and Courgette Skewers with Herb Dressing
intro: Golden, squeaky halloumi and sweet charred courgette threaded onto skewers and drenched in a bright herb dressing. Made for the grill and warm evenings.
ingredients: Handful fresh mint and parsley, chopped
steps: Soak wooden skewers in water if using, then thread on the halloumi, courgette, pepper and onion in alternating pieces.
Brush the skewers with two tablespoons of oil, the oregano, salt and pepper.
Grill or griddle over medium-high heat for twelve to fifteen minutes, turning, until charred and the halloumi is golden.
Whisk the remaining oil with the lemon zest, juice, garlic and chopped herbs to make the dressing.
Season the dressing to taste with a little salt and pepper.
Lay the hot skewers on a platter and spoon the herb dressing generously over the top.
tip: Cut the halloumi a touch larger than the vegetables so it chars nicely without falling off the skewer.

title: Sweet Potato and Black Bean Chilli
intro: A smoky, hearty chilli where soft sweet potato meets earthy black beans and a hit of dark chocolate for depth. Bowl food that only gets better the next day.
ingredients: 2 cans (400 g each) black beans, drained
steps: Soften the onion, garlic and pepper in olive oil over medium heat for about eight minutes.
Stir in the cumin, smoked paprika and cinnamon and cook until fragrant.
Add the sweet potato, black beans and tomatoes with a mug of water and bring to a simmer.
Cover and cook for thirty minutes until the sweet potato is tender, stirring now and then.
Stir in the dark chocolate to melt, then season generously with salt and pepper.
Scatter with fresh coriander and serve with rice, or in bowls with soured cream.
tip: The single square of dark chocolate rounds out the smoky spices; add it right at the end and stir until melted.
